period: c. 1045 BCE (ancient settlement) – present; imperial capital from 1271 CE
title: Beijing
summary: The capital of the People's Republic of China and, before it, of the Mongol, Ming, and Qing empires — a city whose physical layout encodes a specific theory of imperial cosmic order and whose twentieth-century transformation from dynastic capital to communist capital to global megacity is one of the most consequential urban stories in history.
hook: The Forbidden City sits at the center of Beijing, which sits at the center of the empire, which sits at the center of the world, because the emperor is the Son of Heaven and everything radiates from him. That cosmological architecture is still there, and the capital of the People's Republic is built around it.

beginner.thePlace: Beijing is China's capital and one of the world's largest cities, with approximately twenty-two million people in its metropolitan area. It sits on the northeastern edge of the North China Plain, backed by mountains to the north and west, opening to the east and south. The city has been the center of Chinese imperial power for most of the past seven hundred years, and its physical layout — the Forbidden City at the center, surrounded by concentric rings of walls, gates, and zones of decreasing proximity to the imperial person — encodes a specific theory of how the cosmos is organized and where human power stands within it. That layout is still visible today, beneath and alongside the socialist and post-socialist city that has been built around and over it.
beginner.physicalWorld: Beijing's physical geography determined its strategic importance before its cultural significance: it is the point where the North China Plain meets the mountain passes that lead to the Mongolian steppe and Manchuria. Whoever controls Beijing controls the key corridor between the agricultural heartland of China and the northern frontier — which is why both the Mongols (who established Khanbaliq here) and the Manchu (who established the Qing capital here) chose it as their base of operations for governing China. The city's strategic position made it repeatedly the target of conquest and the seat of conquerors. The mountains to the north — which the Great Wall runs along — mark the edge of the steppe frontier that defined China's most persistent strategic challenge.
beginner.theLayers: Beijing's layers are visible in its monuments. The Temple of Heaven — where the emperor performed the annual sacrifice that maintained the cosmic order — dates from the Ming dynasty (early 15th century). The Forbidden City — the imperial palace complex — was built by the Ming and expanded by the Qing. The hutongs (traditional courtyard neighborhoods) that fill the space between the monumental axes were built across the Ming and Qing periods and are still partially inhabited. The socialist layer — Tiananmen Square (expanded massively in 1949–59), the Great Hall of the People, the Monument to the People's Heroes — was superimposed on the imperial axis in the 1950s. The contemporary layer — the CCTV headquarters, the Olympic venues, the ring roads and expressways — was built across the reform era. Each layer is spatially legible; the city is a physical archive of its own political history.
beginner.whatItBecame: Beijing became the ideological capital of the People's Republic of China in a sense that goes beyond its administrative function. Mao Zedong proclaimed the founding of the People's Republic from the Gate of Heavenly Peace (Tiananmen) on October 1, 1949 — deliberately choosing the main gate of the imperial palace as the stage for the communist revolution's founding moment. The portrait of Mao that still hangs over the gate today connects communist authority to the imperial symbolic geography it claimed to have overthrown. Tiananmen Square — enlarged to be the largest public square in the world — replaced the imperial processional space with a space for mass political performance. The communist city was built on the imperial city's bones.
beginner.whoClaimsIt: Multiple claims compete in Beijing's contemporary landscape. The Chinese Communist Party's claim — that the party is the legitimate successor to the revolutionary tradition and that its capital expresses the people's sovereignty — coexists uneasily with the imperial symbolic geography the party inherited and could not discard. Tibetan, Uyghur, Mongolian, and other minority groups exist within a national narrative centered on Beijing that often denies their distinctiveness. The democratic protest tradition — most dramatically represented by the 1989 Tiananmen Square protests — claims the square's public space against the party's monopoly on its symbolic use.
beginner.theLongLife: Contemporary Beijing is a city of twenty-two million people, a global financial center, and the administrative headquarters of the world's second-largest economy. The Olympic Games of 2008 — and again the Winter Olympics of 2022 — were used to present Beijing to the world as a modern global city on Chinese terms. The rapid urbanization and economic growth of the reform era (1978 to present) has transformed the city's physical fabric, replacing hutongs with high-rises and extending the city's footprint across the surrounding plain. The Beijing of the 21st century is simultaneously ancient capital, communist monument, and global megacity — three identities that do not fully cohere.

general.thePlace: Beijing is the most archaeologically layered capital city in the world in the sense that its successive political systems — Mongol empire, Ming dynasty, Qing dynasty, Republic of China, People's Republic — each left legible physical traces that are simultaneously preserved and built over. The Forbidden City is a World Heritage Site surrounded by a communist-era parade ground; the Temple of Heaven is an active tourist destination next to a public park used by elderly residents for morning tai chi. The city's physical layers are not sequenced chronologically from center to periphery in any simple way; they are interpenetrated, with imperial, communist, and contemporary urban forms occupying the same blocks and sometimes the same buildings.
general.physicalWorld: The Forbidden City's design encodes the imperial cosmological theory in physical form. The palace complex is oriented on a strict north-south axis (the axis of the universe, connecting heaven and earth), with the main ceremonial spaces arranged from south to north in order of increasing sacred importance. The emperor faces south (the direction of yang, the positive principle) when seated on the throne; the city spreads out from the palace in concentric zones of decreasing proximity to the sacred center. The Temple of Heaven is to the south (heaven is associated with the south in Chinese cosmology); the Temple of Earth is to the north. The entire city is a spatial diagram of the Chinese cosmic order, with the emperor at the intersection of heaven and earth. This cosmological architecture is not merely decorative; it was the physical expression of the theory of legitimate imperial power.
general.theLayers: The socialist overlay of the 1950s is the most consequential layer in terms of what was lost. The Soviet-advised urban planners of the early People's Republic faced a choice: preserve the imperial city as the capital of the new state, or demolish it and build a modern capital from scratch. They chose preservation but transformation: the Forbidden City became a museum, Tiananmen Square was expanded to seven times its original size, and a new east-west axis (Chang'an Avenue) was cut through the city at right angles to the imperial north-south axis. The urban planner Liang Sicheng — who proposed moving the capital to a new site west of the old city and preserving the imperial city intact — was overruled and later persecuted during the Cultural Revolution. The hutong neighborhoods that filled the old city were partially preserved but partially demolished across the reform era for real estate development, leaving only remnants of the traditional urban fabric.
general.whatItBecame: The 1989 Tiananmen Square protests — and their suppression on June 4th — transformed the square from a domestic political space into an international symbol of democracy denied. The protest movement had used the square's symbolic geography deliberately, occupying the space of the imperial and communist sacred center and claiming it for a different political tradition. The Goddess of Democracy, erected in the square facing the portrait of Mao, was a direct symbolic challenge to the party's monopoly on the space. The suppression — in which hundreds or thousands of protesters were killed — transformed Beijing's symbolic landscape in ways that are still politically sensitive: the Chinese government's systematic suppression of any commemoration of June 4th has made the date and the location some of the most politically charged in Chinese political culture.
general.whoClaimsIt: The contemporary politics of Beijing's symbolic landscape are inseparable from Xi Jinping's political project. The restoration and renovation of the Forbidden City — completed in time for the 2020 opening of the updated Palace Museum — was accompanied by renewed emphasis on Chinese traditional culture as a resource for political legitimacy. The 2021 "museum craze" — in which hundreds of millions of young Chinese visited museums and engaged with traditional culture — was partly organic and partly state-promoted. Xi's explicit invocation of Chinese civilizational continuity (the "Chinese nation" as a five-thousand-year-old civilization) attempts to connect the communist state's legitimacy to the imperial tradition rather than to the revolutionary tradition — a significant shift in ideological emphasis that Beijing's physical landscape was designed to support.
general.theLongLife: The 2008 Olympics transformed Beijing's relationship to global urbanism. The Olympic venues — particularly the Bird's Nest (National Stadium) and the Water Cube, both designed by international architects — were presented as evidence that China had arrived as a global power on its own terms. The 2022 Winter Olympics presented a different face: geopolitical tension, COVID protocols, and a diplomatic boycott by Western countries made the games a site of international confrontation rather than global celebration. Beijing's contemporary identity as a global city is contested in ways that its identity as an imperial capital was not.

advanced.thePlace: Beijing's cosmological design — the Forbidden City at the center of concentric circles of imperial authority — represents one of the most complete examples of what Mircea Eliade called the "axis mundi" in urban form: the physical instantiation of a cosmological theory in which the capital city is the center of the world and the emperor is the pivot between heaven and earth. The design is not merely symbolic but functional: the spatial organization of the city encoded the theory of imperial legitimacy in physical form, making the theory legible to everyone who lived in or visited the city regardless of literacy. The city was the argument for the emperor's cosmic authority. This architectural argument is still partially legible in contemporary Beijing, where the communist party chose to build its own symbolic geography on top of the imperial one rather than replacing it entirely.
advanced.physicalWorld: The destruction of Beijing's hutong neighborhoods — the traditional courtyard housing that filled the space between the monumental axes — represents one of the most consequential acts of urban erasure in modern history. Between the 1950s and the present, approximately 90% of Beijing's hutong fabric has been demolished, displacing hundreds of thousands of long-term residents to make way for high-rise development, infrastructure, and Olympic preparation. The hutongs were not simply residential structures; they were the spatial expression of a particular social organization (the courtyard as extended family unit, the neighborhood as social unit) that the demolition also destroyed. The preservation of the hutongs that remain — now legally protected but economically pressured — is simultaneously a heritage preservation project, a gentrification process (hutong residences are now among the most expensive in Beijing), and a nostalgic performance of a social form that the demolitions have largely ended.
advanced.theLayers: The choice to preserve the Forbidden City rather than demolish it — made against the advice of Soviet advisors who recommended demolition — was one of the most consequential decisions in the history of Chinese urban planning, and its implications were not obvious at the time. The preservation was partly practical (the structures were too massive to quickly demolish), partly ideological (the party chose to appropriate rather than erase the imperial legacy), and partly accidental (Liang Sicheng's preservation advocacy had some effect even as his larger proposals were rejected). The result — a communist capital built around an imperial palace — created a symbolic landscape that subsequent Chinese governments have had to manage: the official narrative claims revolutionary discontinuity with the imperial past while the physical landscape implies continuity. This tension has been managed differently in different periods: the Cultural Revolution attempted to sever the connection (attacking traditional symbols); the reform era and Xi Jinping's era have embraced it (promoting traditional culture as political legitimation).
advanced.whatItBecame: The Tiananmen Square protests of 1989 deserve sustained analysis as an example of how sacred political space is contested. The protesters understood what the Communist Party understood: that occupying Tiananmen Square was not simply a logistical choice but a symbolic claim. The square is the space from which Mao proclaimed the People's Republic; it is the space where the party stages its most important political performances; it is the space that the party's symbolic geography makes the center of the communist state. By occupying it, the protesters were claiming that the space belonged to the people rather than to the party — that the party's symbolic monopoly on "the people's square" could be challenged. The party understood the claim and responded with force. The June 4th suppression was not merely political repression; it was the violent reassertion of symbolic sovereignty over the most politically charged space in China.
advanced.whoClaimsIt: Xi Jinping's ideological project — the "China Dream," the "great rejuvenation of the Chinese nation" — has produced a specific reimagination of Beijing's symbolic landscape. The renovation of the Palace Museum, the development of cultural tourism to traditional sites, and the explicit invocation of Chinese civilizational continuity all serve to ground the party's legitimacy not in the revolutionary tradition alone (Mao, the Long March, the founding of the People's Republic) but in the longer arc of Chinese civilization. This is a significant ideological shift with physical consequences: Beijing is being managed as both a communist capital and a civilizational capital, with the Forbidden City as the physical hinge between these two identities. The tension between these two legitimating frameworks — communist revolution and civilizational continuity — is not resolved; it is managed by keeping both simultaneously available as political resources.
advanced.theLongLife: Beijing's contemporary urban development — particularly the expansion of the city through successive ring roads (now six rings, extending the metropolitan area to vast distances from the original center) and the development of satellite cities — represents one of the most rapid and consequential urban transformations in human history. The city's population grew from approximately 3 million in 1950 to 22 million in 2020; its built-up area expanded approximately tenfold. This transformation has produced a city that is simultaneously one of the most historically significant in the world and one of the most rapidly changing — a combination that creates permanent tension between preservation and development, between the historical landscape and the economic pressures that threaten it.

foundation.theStory: Beijing is the clearest example in the world of a city whose physical layout encodes a specific theory of political legitimacy — and whose successive occupants have had to decide whether to destroy, preserve, or appropriate that encoded theory. The Mongols built on it, the Ming expanded it, the Qing inherited it, the Communists built around it, and Xi Jinping is now using it. Understanding Beijing means understanding how physical urban form carries political meaning across political transformations, and what it means for a revolutionary state to govern from an imperial capital.
foundation.theDebate: The central historical debate concerns the 1950s decision to preserve the Forbidden City rather than demolish it — and whether Liang Sicheng's alternative proposal (move the capital to a new site and preserve the imperial city intact as a historic zone) would have been better for both history and the city's residents. The contemporary debate concerns the demolition of the hutongs — whether the preservation policies have been adequate, whether what has been saved is enough, and what the loss of the hutong fabric means for the city's social and cultural character.
foundation.whyItMatters: Beijing matters because it is the capital of the world's most populous country and second-largest economy, and because its physical landscape encodes the history of that country's political transformations in legible form. Understanding how Beijing works — how its symbolic geography connects communist authority to imperial tradition, how its spatial organization encodes political theory, how its rapid development has destroyed and preserved different layers of its history — is prerequisite to understanding contemporary China.
questions: The Communist Party chose to preserve the Forbidden City and make it the visual backdrop for the founding of the People's Republic, rather than demolishing it as Soviet advisors suggested. What does this choice reveal about the relationship between revolutionary ideology and the physical inheritance of the political orders that revolutions claim to have overthrown? Can a revolution fully break with the past when it governs from the past's capital?

The Tiananmen Square protests of 1989 were deliberately staged in the symbolic center of the communist capital. The suppression was a violent reassertion of symbolic sovereignty over that space. What does this episode reveal about the relationship between physical public space and political legitimacy? Can symbolic geography be permanently claimed, or is it always contested?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: Beijing scholarship spans imperial history (the standard dynastic histories, specialized studies of Ming and Qing institutions), urban history (Hou Renshu's classic study of Ming Beijing, Naquin's Peking: Temples and City Life), and contemporary political and urban studies. Liang Sicheng's writings (translated and collected in various volumes) are essential for understanding the preservation debate of the 1950s. Geremie Barmé's work on contemporary Chinese cultural politics — particularly The Forbidden City (2008) — is the best analysis of how the imperial heritage is being managed in the contemporary period. Ian Johnson's The Souls of China (2017) covers the contemporary religious and cultural revival that Beijing's traditional sites are part of.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The historical evidence for Beijing's physical development is rich: imperial building records, maps (including remarkably detailed Ming-era maps), foreign observer accounts (Marco Polo's description of Khanbaliq, Jesuit accounts of the Qing capital, British diplomatic records), and the archaeological record of the sites themselves. The contemporary evidence is extensive but politically shaped: official Chinese accounts of the city's development are celebratory; critical accounts (of the hutong demolitions, the June 4th suppression) are restricted within China and contested by official sources. The June 4th events are among the best-documented in the history of political repression and among the least accessible to Chinese researchers.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong scholarly argument about Beijing must hold the historical and the contemporary dimensions simultaneously. The imperial legacy is not simply historical context for the contemporary city; it is an active political resource that the Communist Party is currently deploying. The contemporary politics are not simply a modern overlay on a historical substrate; they are reshaping the physical and symbolic landscape in ways that will determine how the historical layers are interpreted by future generations. The most productive analysis treats the relationship between the historical and the contemporary as the central analytical object.
questions: Compare the physical transformation of Beijing under Mao (the expansion of Tiananmen Square, the demolition of the city walls, the construction of the socialist overlay) with the transformation under Xi Jinping (the renovation of the Forbidden City, the promotion of traditional culture, the development of cultural tourism). What different theories of how a capital city should relate to its historical legacy do these two transformation projects encode?

The hutongs of Beijing have been approximately 90% demolished over the past seventy years. The remaining hutongs are now among the most expensive real estate in Beijing — preserved through heritage listing but economically accessible primarily to the wealthy. What does this pattern — preservation through gentrification — suggest about the relationship between heritage conservation and economic development? Is a preserved hutong that only wealthy residents can afford a meaningful preservation of what the hutong was?

research.topic: Forbidden City cosmological design and imperial symbolic geography
research.content: The design principles of the Forbidden City and Beijing's imperial urban layout — the north-south axis, the concentric organization, the cosmological associations of cardinal directions — are well-documented in imperial building records, design manuals, and subsequent scholarly analysis. The relationship between the physical design and the theory of imperial legitimacy it encodes is one of the best-studied examples of cosmological urban planning in world history.

research.topic: Heritage politics and the use of the imperial legacy under Xi Jinping
research.content: Xi Jinping's explicit invocation of Chinese civilizational continuity as a source of political legitimacy — and the physical renovation of heritage sites (particularly the Forbidden City) to support this invocation — represents a significant shift in the Communist Party's relationship to the imperial past. Scholarly analysis of this shift, its implications for Chinese political culture, and its effects on the management of Beijing's physical landscape is emerging rapidly.

research.topic: The June 4th death toll and the full account of the 1989 suppression
research.content: The number of people killed in the Chinese government's suppression of the Tiananmen Square protests on June 3-4, 1989, and in surrounding areas of Beijing, cannot be established with confidence from available evidence. Chinese government sources refuse to provide figures; independent estimates range from hundreds to several thousand. What can be responsibly inferred: a significant number of people were killed by government forces in Beijing on the night of June 3-4, 1989; the suppression involved both troops and civilian protesters in areas surrounding Tiananmen Square as well as in the square itself; the Chinese government's systematic restriction of access to evidence has prevented independent verification. What must remain unknowable unless Chinese archives are opened: the precise casualty figures, the exact sequence of events at specific locations, and the full record of the political decisions that led to the suppression.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Center of the World
comparativeNarrative.content: Within the Chinese imperial cosmological tradition, Beijing was not merely the capital of the empire but the spatial center of the cosmos — the point at which the emperor, as the Son of Heaven, mediated between the heavenly order and the human world. The spatial design of the city encoded this cosmological position: the emperor's throne at the intersection of the north-south axis (heaven-earth axis) and the east-west axis, facing south toward the positive principle; the Temple of Heaven where the emperor performed the annual sacrifice that maintained the cosmic order; the alternating yin-yang colors (the red walls and yellow roofs of the Forbidden City encode these cosmic principles). From within this tradition, Beijing is not simply a large city or a well-positioned capital; it is the physical expression of the moral and cosmic order, and governing from it is the physical act of maintaining that order.

comparativeNarrative.name: Khanbaliq as the Center of Eurasian Empire
comparativeNarrative.content: The Mongols who established Khanbaliq (Great Capital) in the 13th century chose the site partly for its strategic position between the steppe and the agricultural heartland of China, but also because they were building a city that would serve as the capital of an empire spanning from the Pacific to Eastern Europe. Marco Polo's description of Khanbaliq — its planned grid layout, its sophisticated urban infrastructure (the canal system, the granaries, the postal relay network), its multi-ethnic population — suggests a city designed for cosmopolitan imperial rule rather than for specifically Chinese governance. The Mongols' Beijing was a Eurasian imperial capital before it became the specifically Chinese imperial capital that the Ming and Qing dynasties made it. This earlier layer is mostly invisible in the contemporary city but is analytically significant: Beijing's cosmopolitan function predates the specifically Chinese cosmological architecture that has become its defining identity.

comparativeNarrative.name: Beijing as 21st-Century Megacity
comparativeNarrative.content: From the perspective of contemporary global urbanism, Beijing is one of the most rapidly transformed large cities in history — its population grew from 3 million to 22 million in 70 years, its built area expanded tenfold, and its economic structure shifted from industrial socialism to a mixed economy dominated by services, finance, and technology. This transformation has produced a city that is simultaneously one of the most historically significant in the world and one of the most aggressively modern. The tensions this creates — between preservation and development, between the hutong and the high-rise, between the imperial symbolic geography and the demands of a modern megacity — are not unique to Beijing, but they are more concentrated there than anywhere else. Beijing's experience of rapid urbanization while managing extraordinary historical depth is a laboratory for understanding how cities relate to their own pasts under conditions of extreme development pressure.

rabbitHole.label: The Han Dynasty
rabbitHole.reason: The Han established the administrative and ideological framework that subsequent Chinese dynasties — including those that built Beijing — inherited and built upon

rabbitHole.label: Varanasi
rabbitHole.reason: The closest Indian parallel — a city whose physical layout encodes a cosmological theory and whose sacred geography has survived radical political transformations — the comparison illuminates what is distinctive about Beijing's particular case

rabbitHole.label: The Silk Road
rabbitHole.reason: Beijing was the eastern terminus of the Silk Road during the Tang dynasty and the seat of the Mongol empire that unified the routes — the city's position as a global node is inseparable from its position in the Silk Road system

rabbitHole.label: Empire
rabbitHole.reason: Beijing is the physical embodiment of Chinese imperial theory — understanding the concept of empire is prerequisite to understanding what the Forbidden City was designed to express

rabbitHole.label: Paper
rabbitHole.reason: The Chinese bureaucratic state that Beijing was built to govern operated through an extraordinary volume of written documentation — paper was the administrative infrastructure of the imperial system

rabbitHole.label: The Forbidden City — Geremie Barmé (2008)
rabbitHole.reason: The best accessible scholarly treatment of the Forbidden City's history and contemporary management — covers the imperial design, the communist transformation, and the contemporary heritage politics with analytical rigor

reference.author: Susan Naquin
reference.title: Peking: Temples and City Life, 1400–1900
reference.annotation: The most comprehensive scholarly account of Beijing's social and religious life from the Ming through the Qing — covers the temple network, the religious communities, and the urban social structure with extraordinary archival depth. The essential starting point for serious study of imperial Beijing.

reference.author: Geremie Barmé
reference.title: The Forbidden City
reference.annotation: Barmé's history covers the Forbidden City from its construction through the communist transformation to its contemporary management as a heritage site and political symbol. The best accessible treatment of the relationship between the imperial legacy and communist politics.

reference.author: Liang Sicheng (Wilma Fairbank, ed.)
reference.title: A Pictorial History of Chinese Architecture
reference.annotation: Liang's architectural history — the work of the scholar who argued unsuccessfully for the preservation of the entire imperial city — is both the essential scholarly treatment of traditional Chinese architecture and a document of the preservation debate that shaped modern Beijing.

reference.author: Ian Johnson
reference.title: The Souls of China: The Return of Religion After Mao
reference.annotation: Johnson's account of the contemporary religious revival in China — which includes significant material on Beijing's traditional religious sites — provides the essential context for understanding how the city's historical religious landscape is being reactivated in the contemporary period.

commerce.title: The Forbidden City
commerce.author: Geremie Barmé
commerce.note: The most readable scholarly treatment — covers history, politics, and contemporary management in one accessible volume

commerce.title: The Forbidden City: Behind Closed Doors (BBC, 2016)
commerce.note: Three-part BBC documentary with access to the Palace Museum — the best visual introduction to the Forbidden City's physical reality and restoration work

commerce.title: Beijing: From Imperial Capital to Olympic City
commerce.author: Lillian Li, Alison Dray-Novey, and Haili Kong
commerce.note: Comprehensive history of Beijing from imperial origins to the 2008 Olympics — the broadest accessible survey of the city's full historical arc

popularCulture.title: The Last Emperor — Bernardo Bertolucci (1987)
popularCulture.description: Bertolucci's film follows Puyi, the last Qing emperor, from his childhood in the Forbidden City through his abdication, his puppet emperor period under Japan, and his later life as a communist citizen. Filmed in the Forbidden City with extraordinary access, the film is both a historical account and a meditation on how imperial space survives political transformation — Puyi ends his life as a gardener in the city he once ruled from its center.

popularCulture.title: Tiananmen Square 1989
popularCulture.description: The 1989 Tiananmen Square protests — and their suppression on June 4th — are among the most photographed and most censored political events of the 20th century. The image of the "Tank Man" — a lone protester blocking a column of tanks in Chang'an Avenue near the square — is one of the most recognized photographs in history and one of the most systematically suppressed within China. The event transformed Beijing's symbolic landscape in ways that are still politically sensitive and that define the city's global reputation as much as the Forbidden City does.

popularCulture.title: 2008 Beijing Olympics opening ceremony
popularCulture.description: Zhang Yimou's opening ceremony for the 2008 Beijing Olympics — watched by approximately one billion people worldwide — was a deliberate presentation of Chinese civilizational depth and contemporary achievement, using the Bird's Nest stadium as the stage for a performance that moved from ancient Chinese history through the communist period to the contemporary "China Dream." The ceremony was the most watched television event in Chinese history and the most consequential use of Beijing's symbolic geography in the contemporary period.
